title: Americans feel worse about the economy than ever before
summary:
While inflation soars as conflict with Iran drags on and shows up at the gas pump, consumer sentiment is plummeting.
University of Michigan's Consumer Sentiment Index, which tracks how American consumers feel about economic conditions, fell to 47.6 in April — a record low and a nearly 11% decline month-over-month.
The overall sentiment index combines views of how the economy is doing right now with what Americans think is going to happen in the near future, and both crashed this month.
"Much higher gasoline prices, the lower stock market, and volatile news headlines are biting down on consumer sentiment," said Nationwide economist Oren Klachkin.
Do you have a story to share about how rising prices and volatility are affecting your economic outlook?

title: Inflation rose in March to the highest rate in 2 years as the Iran war lifted energy prices
summary:
Inflation climbed to the highest rate since May 2024, and gas prices reached a record month-over-month increase.
Economists expected inflation to rise due to higher energy prices.
Energy prices rose 10.9% over the month, the largest increase since September 2005, after a 0.6% rise in February.
Gas prices surged 21.2% in just a month, the largest on record.
Compared to the previous year, energy prices increased 12.5% in March, the largest rise since November 2022, with gas prices rising 18.9% year-over-year after declining 5.6%.

title: Inflation is expected to rise — and Iran war costs could linger for months despite the ceasefire
summary:
A new inflation report is around the corner, likely unveiling the effects of the Iran war on prices, but even if a tentative ceasefire holds, energy prices could stay high for months.
It's likely that inflation surged in March due to energy prices; annual inflation, as measured by the consumer price index, is expected to rise from 2.4% in February to 3.4% in March, the highest rate since 2024.
In the short term, energy prices could stay volatile.
"The ceasefire had an immediate cooling effect on the spot price of oil, but tensions remain high," Kates said.
It's not just the war that could affect consumer prices; tariffs are still filtering through the economy.

title: Bond Rally Fades as Iran Says Ceasefire Violated
summary:
(Bloomberg) -- Treasuries wiped out an early rally after an Iranian official said the ceasefire deal with the US had been violated.
The ceasefire — after weeks of war — caused oil to plunge, easing some inflation concerns.
Europe LeadsEarlier, US yields fell, trailing steeper yield declines in most European bond markets, reflecting the region’s greater exposure to soaring energy prices.
Short-maturity euro-zone and UK yields plunged earlier as traders slashed wagers on rate hikes by the European Central Bank and Bank of England.
“You can take out more hikes from the European central banks,” Myles Bradshaw, head of global aggregate strategies at JPMorgan Asset Management, said on Bloomberg TV.

title: Buy These 3 ETFs to Guard Against Fresh Volatility, Morningstar Says
summary:
Markets remain volatile after the Iran war ceasefire, but investors have a few options as they hunt for safety from more violent swings, according to Morningstar.
Indeed, oil prices were hovering near $100 again on Thursday after logging their biggest one-day drop since 2020 on Wednesday.
"As we've seen in recent cycles, a wait-and-see approach can leave you sidelined just when markets roar back," Evens said.
Two of them could be held by investors long term, while the third protects investors from "unexpected inflation," Evens added.
"USMV won't keep up during market rallies, but its steady approach makes it a worthwhile ETF in any market environment," he added.

title: Goldman Warns of a 'Very Painful' Gas Shock If Disruptions Persist
summary:
While the war in Iran has oil markets on edge, a natural gas crisis may be brewing, warned a top Goldman Sachs analyst.
The concern centers on Qatar, which supplies about one-fifth of the world's global liquefied natural gas.
But unlike oil, gas markets are highly seasonal.
Natural gas prices have already climbed by 50% to 70%, but Dart said she had expected prices to rally further.
But if it drags on, Goldman sees potential for gas prices to climb another 50% to 100% from current levels as markets are forced to ration demand more aggressively, she added.

title: Video Prices surged in March after oil shock set off by Iran war
summary:
Prices surged in March after oil shock set off by Iran war Inflation surged in March after an oil shock triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, government data showed on Friday.
The inflation report matched economists' expectations.

title: Job seekers haven't felt this dreary about their options since the pandemic
summary:
It's a far cry from the optimism of the pre-pandemic hiring market and the Great Resignation, when opportunities were abundant.
While unemployment remains relatively low, hiring has slowed and it's a tough market for job seekers.
As of March, job seekers spent a median of 11.5 weeks unemployed — nearly two weeks longer than the year prior.
And long-term job seekers are increasingly swelling the ranks of America's unemployed.
And payroll growth was widespread across sectors, another good sign for workers who have been contending with a lopsided hiring market.

title: Prices surged in March after oil shock set off by Iran war
summary:
Prices surged in March after oil shock set off by Iran warFuel prices are displayed at a gas station on April 7, 2026, in Aurora, Ore.Fuel prices are displayed at a gas station on April 7, 2026, in Aurora, Ore.Fuel prices are displayed at a gas station on April 7, 2026, in Aurora, Ore.Fuel prices are displayed at a gas station on April 7, 2026, in Aurora, Ore.Inflation surged in March after an oil shock triggered by the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran, government data showed on Friday.
Prices rose 3.3% in March compared to a year earlier, marking a steep rise from a year-over-year inflation rate of 2.4% in the prior month.
The jump in prices owed in large part to a sharp rise in costs for products impacted by the oil shortage.
U.S. oil prices topped $98 a barrel as of Thursday, standing nearly 50% higher than their pre-war level.
A woman checks gas prices before she fills up her vehicle's tank at a gas station, in Morton Grove, Ill., on April 7, 2026.

title: Applying for Jobs Wasn't Working, so I Started Own Business
summary:
After about nine months of searching for a job, he started a content-creation business.
The problem is that it's much easier to get a job when you're working.
When I started posting videos online, I needed to get this energy out there.
Four or five months ago, I was stuck in my room, applying to jobs, hoping something would land, while my roommates were working and actually having lives.
I am helping this startup run their content systems, and I'm working with a music nonprofit.

title: Oil Shock Warning: Fed Won't Cut Rates to Boost US Economy, Investor Says
summary:
Don't expect the Fed to rush to prop up the US economy if it runs into trouble, Ruchir Sharma says.
The famed investor and Rockefeller Capital Chairman said he believes the Fed's hands are tied when it comes to cutting interest rates to prop up economic growth.
"I think the Fed is out," Sharma said of rate cuts, speaking to CNN this week.
"When you got any shock to the economy, central banks would rush to the aid of the economy at the slightest hint of trouble.
Investors have grown hopeful again about Fed rate cuts since the US announced a ceasefire with Iran, but markets are largely expecting the central bank to remain hawkish.

title: Delta's CEO said affluent passengers, 'immune' to geopolitical conflict, are driving sales
summary:
Delta Air Lines CEO said its affluent customers are bringing in cash for the company at a tricky time in the aviation space.
It's the same story for the Middle East conflict, which has unleashed a massive wave of travel chaos in the region since February and sent fuel prices skyrocketing.
"As difficult as it is to see what's going on with the conflict in the Middle East, I'm not sure that our premium customers are feeling affected by that," the executive added.
It added that its premium revenue grew 14% compared to the same period last year.
Ralph Lauren's CEO Patrice Louvet told Bloomberg in July that his core consumers were "very resilient."

title: Unionized ProPublica staff are on strike over AI, layoffs, and wages
summary:
One of the major issues workers are walking out over is how generative AI will be used at ProPublica — and disclosed to audiences — going forward.
The New York Times, for example, has used AI to help its reporters parse documents related to Jeffrey Epstein; ProPublica reporters used AI tools in their investigation into the rollback of diversity, equity, and inclusion programs at nonprofits.
ProPublica staff have varying opinions on AI in the workforce, Campbell says.
(The union represents both editorial staff like reporters and editors as well as staff working in development and product.)
Some staff see AI as a way to automate tedious tasks, freeing up their time to work on bigger things.

title: The real reason your monthly gas bill keeps going up
summary:
From the cold snap this winter to the US war with Iran, rising energy bills are making headlines.
In 2025, gas utility bills rose 60 percent faster than electric ones and four times faster than inflation, the report found.
In the last decade, gas utility spending on pipes and delivery tripled, reaching $28 billion in 2023, the report notes.
In Minnesota, for example, a new proposed bill would allow gas utilities to build geothermal energy networks in the state, a move that would reduce fossil fuel use.
The bill has received strong support from Minnesota’s largest natural gas utility, CenterPoint Energy, along with labor groups.

title: IMF warns of looming inflation crisis on back of US-Israel war on Iran
summary:
The International Monetary Fund has warned of a potential inflationary crisis with the US-Israel war on Iran darkening the economic outlook, whether or not the fragile ceasefire holds.
IMF Managing Director Kristalina Georgieva said on Thursday that the fund will downgrade its forecast for the world economy next week.
“Had it not been for this shock, we would have been upgrading global growth,” Georgieva said in remarks before next week’s IMF-World Bank Spring Meetings.
In January, the 191-country IMF had upgraded the global growth outlook to 3.3 percent and was poised to do so again when its new forecasts came out next Tuesday.
Georgieva’s comments come a day after the IMF released a new report outlining the economic impact of war on the global economy.
